sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Eye presentations in the prehospital setting range from minor irritation to time-critical threats to vision. The priority is to identify red flags such as chemical injury, penetrating trauma, visual loss, severe pain, corneal ulceration, or orbital infection, while providing safe supportive care for minor presentations.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Redness, irritation, tearing, discharge, or itching
- Pain, photophobia, foreign body sensation, or blepharospasm
- Visual disturbance or difficulty opening the eye
- Swelling, trauma history, or chemical exposure

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Check and document visual acuity where possible before and after treatment
- Contact lens use increases concern for serious corneal infection
- Red flags include reduced visual acuity, severe pain, penetrating injury, chemical splash, corneal opacity or ulcer, irregular pupil, proptosis, hyphaema, recent eye surgery, or suspected herpes infection
- Sudden painful visual loss, marked photophobia, or orbital swelling should prompt urgent escalation

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey and focused assessment of vision and eye appearance
- Remove contact lenses before treatment where possible
- Irrigate chemical or particulate exposure promptly and copiously
- For suspected superficial irritation or conjunctivitis, provide simple supportive care and avoid rubbing the eye
- Shield rather than patch an eye if penetrating injury is suspected
- Prioritise transport over prolonged on-scene assessment if vision is threatened

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider fluorescein examination and topical anaesthetic only within scope and local protocol
- Remove only loose superficial conjunctival foreign bodies if trained and if corneal penetration is not suspected
- Escalate for doctor review or urgent transport for any red-flag eye presentation
- Doctor-directed topical or systemic antimicrobials may be appropriate for selected bacterial or periorbital infections
- Avoid procedures that manipulate a potentially perforated globe

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Do not patch corneal abrasions routinely
- Contact lens wearers with a painful red eye should be treated cautiously because of the risk of serious keratitis
